title: Assembling a National hive box
subtitle: The flat-pack hive that turns up in pieces and what to do with it.
excerpt: A new National hive arrives as a stack of cedar pieces, a bag of nails, and an exploded diagram with no annotations. This covers the brood box, the supers, the floor, the crown board, the roof, the order to glue and nail them, and the bee-space rule that decides every measurement.
sourceNotes: Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All, Internet Archive. National Bee Unit Beekeeper's Toolkit, hive component reference. Thorne's National hive assembly notes (manufacturer's instruction sheet).

glossaryTerms.definition: The deepest box in the hive, where the queen lays and the colony raises young. The National brood box holds eleven standard British frames.
A shallower box added above the brood box during the nectar flow. Bees fill super frames with honey for harvest; the queen is kept out by a queen excluder.
The 6 to 9 mm gap bees leave between combs and between combs and hive walls. Too tight and bees seal it with propolis; too wide and they fill it with brace comb.
The flat board that sits above the top box and below the roof. Holes in the crown board take Porter bee escapes or feeders.
A sticky resin bees collect from tree buds and use to seal small gaps in the hive. Hive tools come out of every inspection coated in it.
Varroa destructor, the parasitic mite that weakens colonies and spreads viruses. Every UK colony has some; the working keeper monitors the count and treats when the threshold is hit.

recipeTools.notes: Useful for scraping any factory burrs from rebates.
Eleven frames per brood box, ten per super.

content.text: A new National hive turns up flat-packed in cedar, with a small bag of galvanised nails and a single-sheet diagram. It looks more complicated than it is. The National is the most common hive in the UK and the pieces fit one way; once the first box is built the rest follow the same pattern. Winter is the right time to build, when the hive sits in a dry workshop with PVA glue going off properly and no urgency to get the bees in.
This walks through a single brood box, a super, the floor, the crown board, and the roof. The same approach applies to a second brood box and to extra supers when you scale up.
What turns up in the box

Lay every piece out on a clean bench and identify what you have. A typical brood-box kit contains four side panels, four corner blocks or finger-jointed ends already cut to rebate, a bag of 30 mm panel nails or oval brads, and a glue line of PVA already factory-applied or supplied separately. The supers are the same construction in a shallower depth. The floor comes as a base panel with two long rails, an entrance block, and a mouse guard. The crown board is one flat sheet of marine ply with two pre-cut holes. The roof is a deeper rim, a flat ply lid, and a sheet of zinc or aluminium for the top.

Count the panels against the parts list before any glue comes out of the bottle. A missing finger-joint side is a quick replacement if you spot it first; halfway through assembly is a poor moment to find a short count.
Building the brood box
Stand the two long sides of the
brood box

up on edge and run a bead of PVA along the finger joints on one end. Push a short side into the joints, tap the finger ends gently with a wooden mallet to seat them flush, and check the corner is square with a try-square. Repeat for the second short side. The box should now stand on the bench as an open-ended rectangle. Run a bead of glue along both top rebates where the frame lugs will sit.

Nail through each finger joint with two 30 mm panel nails per joint, driven so the head sits flush. Avoid nailing through the frame rebate itself; the lugs need a clean shoulder to sit on. Wipe excess glue off with a damp cloth before it sets. Set the box upside down on a flat board, weight it lightly, and leave overnight. PVA will skin in an hour but reaches full strength in twelve to twenty-four hours.
Bee space, and why the rebate matters
The single rule that runs through every National measurement is
bee space

: the 6 to 9 mm gap bees leave between combs and between comb and wall. Top-bee-space hives put the gap above the frames; the National is a bottom-bee-space design, with the gap below the frames between the bottom bars and the box beneath. The frame-lug rebate at the top edge of the box is cut to seat eleven standard British frames so the bottom bars hang at the correct height. If you ever modify a hive box, this is the measurement you protect. Bees seal a gap that is too narrow with
propolis
and fill a gap that is too wide with brace comb; either makes inspections awkward.
Building the super
A
super

is the same construction as the brood box, just shallower. It holds ten shallow frames rather than eleven deep ones; the slightly reduced count gives the bees room to draw deeper comb in the centre frames and store more honey per pull. Glue, square, nail, weight overnight. A working hive needs at least two supers from day one even if only one is on at any time; the second goes on when the first is two thirds full.
Floor and crown board

The floor takes the longest because it carries the most kit. Glue the two long rails to the base panel along their inside edges, square the assembly, and nail through from underneath. The entrance block sits across the front and slides in two positions: open for summer foraging, reduced to a slot for autumn and winter. The mouse guard pins on outside the entrance from October through to March. A
varroa
monitor tray slides on rails beneath the open mesh on modern floors; if your floor is the older solid type, you skip the tray.
The
crown board

is a single sheet of marine ply with two pre-cut feed holes. One hole takes a Porter bee escape when you are clearing supers for harvest; the other is a feed hole for a contact or rapid feeder. Sand any rough edges so the bees do not catch their wings, and seal the ply with raw linseed oil; do not paint the underside, which the bees walk on.

The roof is the simplest piece. Glue and nail the four rim sides to make a deep frame, then nail the flat lid into the rebated top. Fit the metal sheet over the top, fold the edges down, and tack them on. The metal sheets the wood from rain, which is the cause of nearly every wet-hive problem. A second-hand roof with sound metal is fine; a roof with corroded metal lets water through into the colony all winter.
Finishing the wood

Cedar weathers to a silver grey if left untreated and lasts thirty years that way. If you want colour, use a microporous wood stain on outside surfaces only; never on the inside walls or the underside of the crown board. Linseed oil is the traditional choice and works for another long generation. Two coats with a day between, left somewhere airy for a fortnight before bees go anywhere near it. The smell needs to dissipate; a freshly oiled hive smells of oil for a week and a half.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Common assembly snags
attrs.intro: Most problems show up at the corners or with the frame rebate.

items.symptom: Finger joints will not seat fully and the corner is slightly proud.
items.cause: Factory burr left in the joint, or PVA gone slightly off and gripping before the parts seated.
items.fix: Take the joint apart while the glue is still wet, scrape any burr off with a hive tool, run fresh glue, reseat with the mallet. If the glue has gone off, leave it overnight and plane the proud finger flush with a block plane.

items.symptom: Box stands rocking on the bench after assembly.
items.cause: One corner is out of square; the box is a parallelogram, not a rectangle.
items.fix: While glue is still wet, push the diagonally opposite corners together to square the box, check with a try-square, weight it under a flat board overnight. If glue has set, the box will be slightly twisted in service but functional; bees do not mind a millimetre or two of rock.

items.symptom: Frame lugs will not sit flat in the rebate.
items.cause: Nail head proud in the rebate, or the rebate has glue squeeze-out hardening in it.
items.fix: Drive any proud nails flush with a punch. Scrape glue out of the rebate with a hive tool while wet, or chisel it carefully out once set. The lugs need a clean flat shoulder; a bump under one lug throws the comb out of plumb.

items.symptom: Roof leaks at one corner after first rain.
items.cause: Metal sheet not folded tight enough over the edge, or one tack came loose.
items.fix: Bend the metal back down with the flat of a hammer, fold the edge tighter under the rim, drive a fresh tack. A small fold of self-adhesive flashing tape works for an emergency winter fix until you can re-tack properly.

content.text: A National built well in February is ready to take a swarm by May. It will also take a nuc in June or sit as a backup over the summer. The hive lives outdoors for three decades if the wood stays dry under sound metal. The floor wears first, often after twelve to fifteen winters.
